system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 12: Minor Sixth Chords

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 12 — Minor Sixth Chords

_Pages 67-68._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Dorian Mode parent key rule

> The student must remember to relate each Dorian Mede to its parent key before attempting to ap-
> ply the numerical formulas for the formation of the four types of minor chords and before attempting
> to play the scale (dorian mode) of these chords.

**Significance (p. 63).** This is the method's core procedural rule for all minor chord work: always derive the Dorian mode from its parent major key before applying any formula.

## Minor seventh chord formula

> Tones No. 1-3-5-7 of the B Dorian Mode form the B Minor Seventh Chord.

**Significance (p. 67).** Defines the numerical formula for building a minor seventh chord from the Dorian mode.

## Minor ninth chord formula

> Tones No. 1-3-5-7-9 of the B Dorian Mode form the B Minor Ninth Chord.

**Significance (p. 67).** Extends the chord-building formula to the ninth, showing how adding one scale degree transforms a minor seventh into a minor ninth chord.

## Shared triad across minor chord types

> Note that all four chords contain the same triad, B, D, F-Sharp; and a// four chords use the same
> basic scale, the B Dorian Mode, to establish their sound.

**Significance (p. 67).** Establishes the unifying principle that all four minor chord types share a common triad and parent scale.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
